title: Sun Pharma: How a small Kolkata trader became a global buyout artist
summary: Sun Pharma, led by Dilip Shanghvi, is poised for its largest overseas acquisition, a $12.5 billion deal for Organon & Co. This move continues Shanghvi's proven strategy of acquiring companies to fuel growth and global expansion. His approach prioritizes...
thbAction: Use the article as an entry point to pitch THB's commercial visibility layer for territory planning, account prioritization, and next-best-action for pharma leadership teams.
contentHtml:
Dilip Shanghvi, the founder of Sun Pharmaceutical Industries, India's biggest pharma company, is turning another page in his well-thumbed acquisitions playbook. ET has reported based on information from sources that Sun Pharma is set to make its biggest and most ambitious overseas acquisition till date, a near $12.5 billion acquisition of US-based Organon & Co. This will also be the largest overseas purchase by an Indian company after the British steel company Corus was taken over by Tata Steel in 2007.

The Organon deal, if it happens, will be yet another piece in Shaghvi's acquisitions strategy with which he grew his small trading operation in Kolkata’s wholesale medicine market into India's most valued pharma company over four decades: using acquisitions not just to grow, but to transform Sun Pharma into a global pharmaceutical force.

Also Read: Sun Pharma to acquire Organon for $12.5 bn, its biggest till date

Shanghvi's acquisition playbook

Unlike many Indian pharmaceutical companies that built scale primarily through organic growth, Sun Pharma’s rise has been disproportionately driven by acquisitions. Shanghvi’s approach has not been about buying for size alone. It has been about buying for strategic advantage, market access and turnaround potential.

Over the years, Sun Pharma’s acquisition strategy has followed several patterns. The first is the acquisition of distressed or weak companies with strong product pipelines or geographic presence. These assets often come with operational inefficiencies or regulatory challenges, which Sun Pharma attempts to resolve.Another is the acquisition of specialty businesses that strengthen Sun Pharma’s position in niche therapeutic areas, particularly dermatology, oncology and ophthalmology in regulated markets like the US. Yet another is incremental stake building rather than outright hostile takeovers. Shanghvi has often preferred patience over aggression, gradually increasing control when valuations align with his long term view.

This framework is best understood through two landmark deals that reshaped Sun Pharma’s global identity -- Ranbaxy and Taro of Israel.

Also Read: Sun Pharmaceutical to adopt a 'disciplined' approach to M&A: Dilip Shanghvi

Ranbaxy, the high risk turnaround

The acquisition of Ranbaxy Laboratories in 2014 remains the most consequential deal in Sun Pharma’s history. It was not just large in size but also complex in structure and execution. Ranbaxy was acquired from Daiichi Sankyo in a deal that was driven largely by regulatory and financial distress. At the time, Ranbaxy was struggling with serious compliance issues in key markets, especially the US, which had impacted its reputation and financial stability.

Shanghvi saw what others were hesitant to engage with. Instead of avoiding the risk, he treated Ranbaxy as a turnaround opportunity with global strategic value. The acquisition instantly made Sun Pharma the largest pharmaceutical company in India and significantly expanded its footprint in international generics. However, the real challenge was not acquisition but integration. Sun Pharma had to deal with regulatory cleanups, operational restructuring and product rationalisation. The Ranbaxy deal was as much about discipline as it was about ambition.

The key strategic lesson from Ranbaxy was that Shanghvi was willing to absorb short0term uncertainty if the long term asset value justified it. It also reinforced Sun Pharma’s identity as a company capable of fixing complex businesses rather than simply acquiring clean ones.

Taro, patience as a strategic weapon

If Ranbaxy acquisition was about scale and risk, Taro Pharmaceuticals buyout was about patience and precision. Sun Pharma’s engagement with Taro began long before full control was achieved. The company gradually built its stake over several years, navigating shareholder dynamics, regulatory considerations and valuation disagreements.

Taro’s strength lay in dermatology products in the US, a high margin and relatively stable therapeutic segment. However, the structure of the acquisition was deliberately slow and incremental. Sun Pharma did not rush to acquire full ownership. Instead it built influence over time until a final consolidation became both financially and strategically optimal. This approach allowed Sun Pharma to avoid overpaying while steadily aligning operations. The full acquisition ultimately gave Sun Pharma tighter control over its dermatology business in the US market and improved margin visibility in a key specialty segment.

The Taro deal underlined a very different dimension of Shanghvi’s acquisition philosophy compared to Ranbaxy. It showed that patience itself can be a competitive advantage when combined with financial discipline.

Other key acquisitions that built global scale

Beyond Ranbaxy and Taro, Sun Pharma’s rise has been shaped by a series of strategic acquisitions that collectively expanded its geographic reach, product depth and specialty positioning. One of the earliest and most important international steps was the acquisition of Caraco Pharmaceutical Laboratories in the US. This deal gave Sun Pharma a direct manufacturing and distribution base in the US generics market. At a time when most Indian pharma companies were still export oriented from India, Caraco provided Sun Pharma with a local American footprint, regulatory familiarity and a platform to navigate USFDA systems more effectively.

Another significant move was the acquisition of Chattem Chemicals, which strengthened Sun Pharma’s presence in over the counter and specialty chemicals linked to pharmaceutical formulations. This helped diversify its portfolio beyond pure prescription generics and added adjacency to consumer health products in the US market. Sun Pharma also acquired URL Pharma, a deal that significantly expanded its branded generics portfolio in the US. URL Pharma brought established products with strong brand recognition in therapeutic areas such as gout and pain management. This acquisition helped Sun Pharma move slightly closer to branded specialty positioning rather than being limited to unbranded generics.

In Europe, Sun Pharma expanded its presence through the acquisition of Ocular Technologies, which strengthened its capabilities in ophthalmic products. This was strategically important because ophthalmology later became one of Sun Pharma’s key specialty segments globally. The acquisition added both technical expertise and a differentiated product pipeline in a regulated market.

Another important acquisition was Dusa Pharmaceuticals, which enhanced Sun Pharma’s dermatology and photodynamic therapy portfolio in the US. This deal was strategically aligned with Sun Pharma’s long term push into dermatology, a high margin specialty segment that eventually became central to its global identity. Dusa’s product line helped Sun Pharma build credibility in advanced dermatological treatments beyond generics. Sun Pharma also acquired Pharmalucence, a US based injectable pharmaceutical company. This acquisition strengthened its presence in injectable products used in oncology and diagnostic imaging. It also improved Sun Pharma’s capability in sterile manufacturing, which is one of the more complex and regulated segments in global pharmaceuticals.

Across all these acquisitions, a consistent pattern emerges. Each deal was not about size alone but about filling a specific strategic gap, whether it was geographic access, regulatory capability, specialty therapy depth or manufacturing complexity. Together, they formed the stepping stones that allowed Sun Pharma to transition from a domestic Indian manufacturer into a diversified global pharmaceutical company with a strong presence in regulated markets.

Core strategies beyond acquisitions

While acquisitions form the most visible part of Sun Pharma’s growth story, they are only one component of Shanghvi’s broader business philosophy. One of his core strategies has been extreme focus on profitability over revenue growth. Sun Pharma has often prioritised margins and cash flow discipline over aggressive top line expansion. Another key strategy is concentration in therapeutic expertise. Rather than diversifying widely, Sun Pharma has built strength in select areas such as dermatology, oncology and chronic therapies. This focus has allowed it to develop deeper capabilities in regulatory compliance and product development.

The company prioritises operational simplicity. Sun Pharma has a relatively lean decision-making structure compared to global peers. Strategic decisions are typically centralised, and execution is tightly controlled. An often underappreciated strategy is regulatory caution. After experiences like Ranbaxy, Sun Pharma has placed significant emphasis on compliance systems, especially in regulated markets like the US. These strategies create a framework where acquisitions are not isolated events but part of a tightly controlled growth architecture.

From Kolkata’s Dawa Bazaar to global boardrooms

Long before billion dollar acquisitions, Dilip Shanghvi’s business journey began in a far more modest environment. He started in Kolkata’s Dawa Bazaar, working in the pharmaceutical distribution trade alongside his father. Shanghvi joined his father's pharma distribution business in Kolkata even before he graduated.

The early phase of his career was not defined by manufacturing ambition but by market understanding. He gained deep exposure to how medicines moved through India’s fragmented supply chain, how pricing worked and how demand behaved across different therapeutic categories.

Sun Pharma was founded in 1983 with a relatively small capital base -- Shanghvi borrowed Rs 10,000 from his father. The initial focus was not on scale but on profitability in select psychiatric medicines. This focus on niche, high-margin therapeutic areas became a defining feature of the company’s strategy over time. As the business expanded, Shanghvi gradually shifted from a domestic manufacturing model to a global generics mindset. The transition was not abrupt. It was built through careful reinvestment, incremental scaling and selective acquisitions that aligned with his core principle of controlled expansion.

Dilip Shanghvi, the man behind the game

Shanghvi’s personal style is closely aligned with his business decisions. He is known for being extremely private, avoiding public attention and rarely engaging in media visibility. The 70-year-old would count as among the most low-profile Indian business tycoons today, rarely seen at parties and zealously guarding his privacy. He is known for his unassuming, soft spoken demeanor and parsimony with words.

His decision making style is often described as analytical and restrained. He is not known for impulsive expansion or aggressive public positioning. Instead, he tends to evaluate opportunities over long time horizons and prioritises stability over speed. This temperament reflects in Sun Pharma’s acquisition history. Even large deals have typically been followed by long integration phases rather than rapid restructuring announcements.

Another defining trait is his preference for delegation with control. While Sun Pharma has grown into a global organisation, strategic control remains tightly held, ensuring consistency in decision-making. Shanghvi studied commerce but he can stand his ground against research scientists in his industry on therapeutic areas or even different pathways to get to the same molecule.
